metadata.title: Fundamentals of Game Design, 3rd Ed. (Adams) — Book Review
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Seth Brown
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Adams distinguishes toys with no goal or rules, puzzles with a goal but no rules, and games with both. A Bateman 2008 survey of over 1000 players documents ten distinct emotions games evoke. The structural analysis of story types is rigorous, well-illustrated, and analytically strong. Combining story structures yields roughly a billion possible plots, most of which no one has built.

artifactLines: Adams distinguishes toys with no goal or rules, puzzles with a goal but no rules, and games with both.
A Bateman 2008 survey of over 1000 players documents ten distinct emotions games evoke.
The structural analysis of story types is rigorous, well-illustrated, and analytically strong.
Combining story structures yields roughly a billion possible plots, most of which no one has built.

beats.narration_text: The taxonomy separating toys (no goal, no rules), puzzles (goal, no rules), and games (both), plus the claim that story-type combinations yield 'approximately one billion plots' no one has built, really sharpened my thinking. Help me push further: (1) test the toy-puzzle-game distinction against a borderline case like an open-world sandbox; (2) explain how the structural story analysis reaches a billion-plot count; (3) reason about why, with that space, no one has built the untried game.
